original_hypothesis.title: Premature Validator Whitelist Removal via Chain ID Change on L2 Deployments
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: removeWhitelistAfterFork() permanently disables the validator whitelist if block.chainid differs from the immutable deployTimeChainId. On L2 chains (Arbitrum chains targeting their own L1), protocol upgrades may change the chain ID. If the rollup contract is deployed on an L2 that later undergoes a chain ID change (upgrade, hard fork, testnet reconfiguration), any unprivileged user can permanently disable the validator whitelist, allowing anyone to become a validator and create assertions or layer zero edges without being whitelisted.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The function has no admin override or recovery mechanism. Once validatorWhitelistDisabled is set to true, it cannot be reversed (no setter exists in RollupUserLogic). The deployment context shows target chains include Arbitrum L2s which may undergo chain ID changes during protocol upgrades.
deployment_assumptions: Rollup is deployed on a chain that may undergo a chain ID change
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: hardhat.config.ts shows deployment targets include arbitrumOne, nova, and custom chains. Documentation states target chains: Ethereum, Arbitrum. L2 chain ID changes during protocol upgrades are possible.

proof_of_signal.pattern: removeWhitelistAfterFork() checks _chainIdChanged() which compares block.chainid to immutable deployTimeChainId. Permanently sets validatorWhitelistDisabled = true with no recovery path.

preconditions: Rollup is deployed on a chain whose chain ID may change
The chain undergoes a hard fork or upgrade changing its chain ID

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: deployTimeChainId = block.chainid (set at deploy time, immutable); _chainIdChanged() = deployTimeChainId != block.chainid
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: After chain ID change, anyone calls removeWhitelistAfterFork() to permanently disable whitelist
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker gains unrestricted validator access to create assertions and challenge edges without being whitelisted, bypassing economic gatekeeping

economic_questions: Which deployment chains could undergo chain ID changes?
What is the economic impact of whitelist removal (spam assertions, delay attacks)?
Can the admin re-enable the whitelist after removal?
validation_requirements: Check if any admin function exists to re-enable the whitelist (not visible in provided code)
Verify deployment targets and their chain ID stability
Assess cost of spam assertions without whitelist

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Only gas cost for the removeWhitelistAfterFork call
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Indirect: enables spam assertions, delay attacks, or stake-free challenge participation
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Enables further attacks rather than direct profit
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Single transaction after chain ID change

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Immediately after a chain ID change event
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Opens validator participation to all, potentially enabling griefing

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All rollup users relying on validator whitelist for security
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Indirect: increased risk of invalid assertions and delays

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Deployment chain ID stability, admin recovery mechanisms
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify no admin setter for validatorWhitelistDisabled exists in RollupAdminLogic

validation_result.impact_summary: The function behaves exactly as intended to prevent rollup stalling during a hard fork.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The hypothesis claims that `removeWhitelistAfterFork()` contains a vulnerability because it allows any user to permanently disable the validator whitelist if the chain ID changes. However, this is not a code defect; it is an explicitly intended safety mechanism.

In the event of a network hard fork (which results in a new chain ID), the original whitelisted validators might abandon the new fork. To prevent the rollup from permanently halting on the new fork, `removeWhitelistAfterFork()` allows anyone to disable the whitelist, decentralizing the validator set and allowing the rollup to continue operating.

The hypothesis also incorrectly assumes that L2 protocol upgrades (like Arbitrum upgrades) change the chain ID. Standard EVM and Arbitrum protocol upgrades (such as the Arbitrum Classic to Nitro upgrade) do not change the chain ID. A chain ID change strictly implies a hard fork or a completely new network, in which case the whitelist removal is the desired protocol behavior. The lack of an admin recovery path is also intentional, as the admin might not support the fork.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The function `removeWhitelistAfterFork()` in `RollupUserLogic.sol` explicitly checks `_chainIdChanged()` and sets `validatorWhitelistDisabled = true`. The naming of the function and its logic clearly demonstrate that this is an intended feature for handling forks, not a vulnerability.

deployment_assumptions_checked: Rollup is deployed on a chain that may undergo a chain ID change
config_validation.deployment_evidence: Deployment scripts and hardhat config show standard EVM/Arbitrum networks (arbitrumOne, nova). These networks do not change chain IDs during protocol upgrades.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The described scenario is the intended use case of the function. If a chain forks and changes its chain ID, the protocol intentionally allows anyone to disable the whitelist to preserve liveness.
